title: Your First Materials Investigation: What Is This Made Of?
description: Investigate the materials around you through observation and simple tests. This session spans material properties, materials families, and manufacturing thinking.

steps.instruction: Collect 5 objects made of different materials (metal spoon, plastic pen, glass, wood, fabric). Read about mechanical properties: for each object, estimate its hardness, stiffness, and ductility from how it feels.
steps.expectedOutcome: You can rank the 5 materials by hardness and stiffness, and identify which are ductile (can bend without breaking) versus brittle
steps.hint: Scratch resistance indicates hardness; resistance to bending indicates stiffness; ability to bend without breaking is ductility

steps.instruction: Sort your objects by material family: metal, polymer, ceramic, composite? Read about metals, polymers, and ceramics: what are the characteristic properties that define each family?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can correctly categorize each object and explain the family characteristics — metals conduct electricity; polymers are lightweight; ceramics are hard and brittle
steps.hint: When unsure, test electrical conductivity with a battery, wire, and LED — metals conduct, polymers and ceramics don't

steps.instruction: For each object, guess how it was made: cast/molded, machined, or formed? Read about casting/molding and machining: what shapes are easily produced by each process?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can match each object to a likely manufacturing process based on its shape, surface finish, and material, and explain why that process is appropriate
steps.hint: Complex shapes with internal features suggest casting/molding; precise flat surfaces suggest machining; uniform cross-sections suggest extrusion

steps.instruction: Pick the plastic object. Read about life cycle assessment: trace its life from raw material to end-of-life. Where are the biggest environmental impacts?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can trace raw material extraction → processing → manufacturing → use → disposal and identify which phase has the largest impact for plastic objects
steps.hint: For most plastics, raw material extraction (petroleum) and end-of-life disposal (often landfill or ocean) are the highest-impact phases

steps.instruction: Consider the plastic object: could it be designed to be more circular — easier to repair, reuse, or recycle? Read about circular economy: what design changes would improve circularity?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can propose at least one design change that would improve end-of-life recyclability or repairability, and explain the circular economy principle it embodies
steps.hint: Monomaterial design (one material type rather than glued-together combinations) is the biggest enabler of recyclability
